eli3: Seasonality is a systematic, periodic pattern in a time series that repeats at regular intervals (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annually). It is distinct from trend (long-run direction) and irregular fluctuations (random noise). Example: retail sales spike every December, flu incidence peaks in winter, traffic is highest on weekday mornings — these are seasonal patterns.

Seasonal decomposition separates a series into three components: Yₜ = Tₜ + Sₜ + Rₜ (additive) or Yₜ = Tₜ · Sₜ · Rₜ (multiplicative). The additive model is appropriate when the seasonal amplitude is constant regardless of the trend level; the multiplicative model is appropriate when the seasonal amplitude grows proportionally with the trend (common for economic and financial series).

Seasonal adjustment removes the seasonal component to reveal the underlying trend and cycle — essential for comparing months or quarters that differ in 'expected' seasonal behavior (e.g., unemployment in January vs July). Major statistical agencies (BLS, Eurostat) use X-13ARIMA-SEATS or STL decomposition for official seasonal adjustment of GDP, unemployment, and CPI.

whenWhy.title: Decomposition for understanding and visualization
whenWhy.body: STL (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess, Cleveland et al. 1990) decomposes Yₜ = Tₜ + Sₜ + Rₜ using locally weighted regression (loess). Advantages over classical decomposition: handles any seasonal period, robust to outliers, seasonal component can change over time. Classical decomposition uses moving averages for the trend — inflexible and cannot handle changing seasonality.

whenWhy.title: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) modeling
whenWhy.body: SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s explicitly models seasonal autocorrelation. Seasonal differencing ∇ˢYₜ = Yₜ − Yₜ₋ₛ removes seasonal non-stationarity. The 'airline model' SARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1)₁₂ is a parsimonious but effective model for monthly economic data. For weekly data with multiple seasonal periods (e.g., daily, weekly, annual cycles), TBATS or Prophet handles multiple seasonalities.

whenWhy.title: Seasonal dummy variables in regression
whenWhy.body: For regression with seasonal data: include K−1 seasonal dummy variables (K = number of seasons, drop one to avoid multicollinearity). For monthly data: 11 monthly dummies. Dummy coefficient interpretation: seasonal effect relative to the omitted reference month. For multiplicative seasonality: log-transform Yₜ and use additive dummies on the log scale. More flexible than fixed seasonal dummies: Fourier series terms sin(2πkt/s), cos(2πkt/s) for k = 1,…,K/2.

deepDiveIntro: In additive decomposition the series equals the sum of trend, seasonal, and irregular components; in multiplicative decomposition it equals their product, which is equivalent to additive decomposition after taking logarithms. The seasonal component is constrained to average to zero over each full cycle so it does not absorb level shifts. Classical decomposition estimates the trend with a moving average, subtracts it to isolate the seasonal and irregular parts, then averages within each season to estimate the seasonal component.

STL decomposition iterates between a loess smoother applied within each season across time and a loess smoother applied to the seasonally adjusted series, controlled by a seasonal window and a trend window parameter. Larger windows produce smoother components. An optional robustness pass down-weights observations with large residuals, making STL resistant to outliers. After decomposition, the seasonally adjusted series can be forecast with any standard method and the seasonal component added back. [1]
formula: Additive: Yₜ = Tₜ + Sₜ + Rₜ;  Multiplicative: Yₜ = Tₜ · Sₜ · Rₜ

deepDive.heading: Classical Decomposition
deepDive.body: Classical decomposition separates a series into trend, seasonal pattern, and irregular remainder by first smoothing the long-run movement and then averaging seasonal effects. It is easy to understand and works well for stable, regular patterns. Its main weakness is that it assumes the seasonal pattern does not change much over time.
deepDive.formula: Additive: Yₜ = Tₜ + Sₜ + Rₜ;   Multiplicative: Yₜ = Tₜ · Sₜ · Rₜ

deepDive.heading: STL Decomposition
deepDive.body: STL decomposition is a more flexible alternative that uses local smoothing instead of rigid moving-average formulas. It can handle changing seasonal patterns and is more resistant to outliers than classical decomposition. That flexibility is why it has become a standard exploratory tool for modern seasonal time series.

deepDive.heading: Seasonal ARIMA: SARIMA
deepDive.body: Seasonal ARIMA extends ordinary ARIMA by adding terms that model dependence across seasonal lags such as twelve months or four quarters. Seasonal differencing removes recurring level shifts, while seasonal autoregressive and moving-average terms capture repeated dynamics at those longer lags. This makes SARIMA one of the main workhorse models for regularly seasonal data.

deepDive.heading: Fourier Terms for Flexible Seasonality
deepDive.body: Fourier terms provide a smooth way to model seasonality with a small number of parameters. They are especially useful when the seasonal pattern is complex, long, or when multiple seasonal cycles must be represented at once. This idea is widely used in modern forecasting systems because it scales much better than large sets of seasonal dummy variables.

takeaways: Additive decomposition: Yₜ = Tₜ + Sₜ + Rₜ; use when seasonal amplitude is constant. [1]
Multiplicative: Yₜ = Tₜ·Sₜ·Rₜ (or log-transform → additive); use when amplitude grows with trend. [1]
STL: robust loess-based decomposition; handles changing seasonality and any seasonal period. [1]
SARIMA: add seasonal AR(P), MA(Q), and seasonal differencing (D) to ARIMA. [1]
Fourier terms: K pairs of sin/cos allow flexible seasonal patterns with fewer parameters than dummies. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle multiple seasonal periods (e.g., hourly data with daily and weekly seasonality)?
edgeCases.a: Standard ARIMA/SARIMA handles only one seasonal period. For multiple seasonalities: (1) TBATS (De Livera et al. 2011): models multiple seasonal periods with trigonometric Fourier terms, Box-Cox transformation, and ARMA errors — handles complex seasonality automatically; (2) Prophet (Facebook/Meta): uses Fourier series for trend, yearly, weekly, and custom seasonalities, with changepoints for trend; (3) STL with multiple seasonal periods (MSTL): iterative decomposition removing each seasonal component in turn. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the X-13ARIMA-SEATS method used by statistical agencies?
edgeCases.a: X-13ARIMA-SEATS is the US Census Bureau's seasonal adjustment software, combining two approaches: (1) X-11: filter-based multiplicative decomposition using Henderson moving averages — no parametric model assumptions, robust; (2) SEATS: signal extraction based on ARIMA model — projects component models from an estimated ARIMA. X-13 first identifies an ARIMA model (RegARIMA step, allowing for calendar effects, outliers, and regressors), then applies X-11 or SEATS for decomposition. Eurostat uses X-13ARIMA-SEATS for all EU official statistics. [1]

sources.label: Cleveland et al. (1990) — STL: A Seasonal-Trend Decomposition Procedure Based on Loess, J. Official Statistics 6(1)
Hyndman & Athanasopoulos — Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.), Ch. 3, 9
